title: Vector Storage Technology Selection
context: NEURAL SYNC requires vector similarity search for behavioral embeddings (work_style, motivation_vector) and skill embeddings to power the five-dimension matching engine. The matching engine must support ANN (approximate nearest-neighbor) search across up to 10,000+ developer profiles with sub-150ms query latency. The choice of vector storage affects: (1) operational complexity — a separate vector service multiplies infra burden; (2) GDPR erasure atomicity — purging embeddings alongside structured profiles is simpler when both live in the same DB; (3) horizontal scalability path; and (4) Phase 2 migration cost when external data sources (Git, Slack, HR) are added. The tech stack mandates PostgreSQL as the relational store, making a pgvector co-location option naturally available.

options_considered.name: pgvector extension on PostgreSQL
pros: Zero additional services — vector data lives in the same PostgreSQL instance as structured profiles, eliminating a separate operational surface
GDPR erasure is fully atomic: DELETE cascade in a single PostgreSQL transaction covers both structured rows and their embeddings
HNSW index (available since pgvector 0.5.0) provides sub-millisecond ANN search well within 10K–500K vector range
Alembic migrations manage both relational schema and vector table schema under a single toolchain
Phase 2 migration to managed Postgres (Supabase, Neon, AWS RDS with pgvector) preserves 100% SQL compatibility
ACID transactions include vector reads — no eventual-consistency windows between profile and embedding state
No per-query network hop to an external service; local socket / connection-pool round-trip only
cons: PostgreSQL DBA must enable the pgvector extension (single-line ALTER); minor setup step
Maximum scale ceiling is lower than purpose-built managed services (practical limit ~10M vectors before requiring sharding)
No dedicated monitoring dashboard for vector query performance; must instrument via pg_stat_statements
HNSW index build time increases with dataset size (mitigated by offline index rebuilds)

options_considered.name: Chroma (in-process embedded vector DB)
pros: Zero-configuration for local development; pip-installable Python library
Native Python API with metadata filtering
Fast iteration in CI without external service dependency
cons: Separate persistence layer from PostgreSQL: GDPR erasure requires two separate delete operations with no cross-store transaction, creating atomicity gap
Not production-hardened for high-concurrency async workloads at scale
No native horizontal scaling for the vector store; single-process bottleneck
Adds operational complexity when deploying: Chroma persistent storage must be volume-mounted separately from Postgres data
Phase 2 migration to managed vector DB requires data re-export and re-import

options_considered.name: Pinecone (managed vector service)
pros: Unlimited horizontal scale; purpose-built for ANN at billions of vectors
cons: External SaaS dependency introduces network latency (~20–100ms per query) compared to local pgvector socket call
GDPR erasure requires synchronous HTTP call to Pinecone API outside the PostgreSQL transaction; partial failure leaves dangling embeddings
Monthly cost adds up for MVP with small dataset; no free tier for production throughput
Vendor lock-in; migration away requires full re-export of all embedding vectors
No offline/CI mode without mocking the Pinecone HTTP API
Out-of-scope for Phase 1 per requirements: 'Production-scale vector DB managed service deployment deferred to Phase 2'

decision: Use pgvector extension on the existing PostgreSQL instance. Two tables are added to the PostgreSQL schema: developer_embeddings (developer_id FK, embedding_type ENUM('skill','behavioral'), vector VECTOR(1536), created_at) and project_embeddings (project_id FK, embedding_type ENUM('skill'), vector VECTOR(1536), created_at). An HNSW index with cosine distance operator is created on each embedding column. The embedding dimension is 1536, matching the output dimension of the text-embedding-3-small model (or a local sentence-transformer if cost is a concern). All GDPR erasure cascades are handled within a single PostgreSQL transaction covering both relational rows and vector rows, ensuring atomicity. If the project scales beyond 1M developer profiles, the migration path is to Supabase or AWS RDS with pgvector — no application code changes required beyond the connection string.
consequences: The embedding-worker background task writes vectors to pgvector tables via the same asyncpg connection pool used by the relational service. The matching engine issues a single SQL query joining developer_embeddings with DeveloperProfile to retrieve candidates ranked by cosine similarity, then applies the five-dimension weighted scoring formula in Python. GDPR cascade DELETE is a single SQL transaction. Developers and reviewers do not need to operate or monitor a second data store in Phase 1. The HNSW index must be rebuilt offline when the embedding model is updated (Phase 2 concern). Index build for 10,000 vectors completes in under 30 seconds on commodity hardware.
